sentence: Subjective:
The patient is a male who suffered a right knee injury three days ago while playing soccer, specifically feeling a pop in his knee during a plank kick.
He reports swelling, limited mobility, and pain on a scale of 6-7 out of 10.
He has been taking ibuprofen, which provided some relief, but he is concerned about his knee's functionality and ability to bear weight.
He denies any fever or other symptoms.
The patient has a history of using ibuprofen regularly for pain relief.
Past medical history:
The patient's past medical history is not mentioned.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any other symptoms in other organs.
Current medications:
I prescribed ibuprofen [dosage not specified] [frequency not specified] for pain relief.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
The patient has a marked limp on the right leg upon ambulation.
There is significant effusion in the right knee with a slight bit of ecchymosis.
Palpation at the medial joint line causes some pain.
The patient has difficulty with passive range of motion, lacking a few degrees of terminal extension to about 115 degrees and lacking about 20-30 degrees of terminal flexion due to pain.
The patient has a positive Lachman exam.
The patient's bilateral lower extremities are neurovascularly intact, with muscle strength of 5 out of 5.
Test Results:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has a right knee injury suggestive of an anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tear.
The patient's knee is currently active; the status of the ACL is suspected.
Plan:
I ordered an MRI to evaluate the integrity of the ACL.
I prescribed meloxicam 50 milligrams per day to reduce swelling and pain.
I instructed the patient to ice the knee if necessary, wear a knee immobilizer, and use crutches to avoid putting too much stress and strain on the knee.
I advised the patient to limit their activities, including work activities, and to avoid jogging until further notice.
I also instructed the patient to move their leg slightly every now and then to prevent it from locking up.
Follow-up:
The patient will have an MRI, and I will reevaluate the results before determining the next course of action.
